text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon entering the abdominal cavity and dissecting adhesions between the gastric remnant and the major omentum. Short gastric vessels are identified and taken down, allowing mobilization of the stomach up to the gastroesophageal junction. The gastric remnant, pouch, and alimentary limb are clearly dissected, and the distal part of the gastric remnant is transected at the identified area of stenosis.

The surgeon elevates the gastric remnant while ensuring the viability of the left gastric artery to maintain blood supply to the pouch. Using a harmonic scalpel, adhesions to the remnant are dissected until the gastrojejunostomy is clearly identified. The gastrojejunostomy and the distal part of the gastric remnant are divided en bloc.

The surgeon gains control of the gastric remnant and identifies the left gastric artery on the pouch. The pouch is transected proximal to the stenotic gastro-degenerostomy using green cartridge linear staplers, suitable for thickened gastric tissue. Adhesions of the alimentary limb to the mesenterium are lysed, allowing it to be positioned in the upper abdomen in an anti-colic, anti-gastric manner without tension. The staple lines are oversewn with running vial sutures.

The surgeon reinforces all staple lines at the gastric stump and uses clip-up pliers to improve hemostasis. A side-to-side gastroenterostomy is performed to reconstruct the upper portion of the gastrointestinal tract.

The surgeon performs an enterotomy at the midpoint between the mesenterium and the anti-mesenteric border of the small bowel. Using a blue cartridge linear stapler, a side-to-side gastro-degenerostomy is created on the posterior wall. The gastrotomy is assisted by an E-wall tube, and the anterior wall is completed with a double layer of rendezvous sutures.

The surgeon performs a full-thickness running vial suture, starting from the proximal end and moving distally, then returning to lock the distal part of the suture to the proximal part. Routine intraoperative EGDs are conducted, and leak tests are demonstrated using air and methylene blue.

Drains are placed routinely in our primary and reoperative procedures. While in primary procedures, drains are removed before patients are discharged home. In performing reoperative surgery, the drains are left in place for at least ten to fourteen days. Feeding gastrostomies are used only in cases when gastroenterogenostomies are not
